coredata.dc:title: Pesticide-derived bright chlorine-doped carbon dots for selective determination and intracellular imaging of Fe(III)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yaqin
Man, Yun
Li, Sisheng
Wu, Shibiao
Zhao, Xiaoli
Xie, Fazhi
Qu, Qishu
Zou, Wen-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by the conversion from organics or biomass to fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots), the use of pesticide 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) as a precursor to prepare C-dots has been reported. The as-prepared chlorine-doped C-dots display a brightly blue emission at ∼445 nm with ∼22.8% quantum yield. Also, the surface of C-dots enriches functional groups, such as phenolic hydroxyl and carboxylic acid, etc., which can capture ferric ion (Fe(III)), resulting in the quenching of blue fluorescence of C-dots through an inner filter effect. The quantitative assay for Fe(III) was therefore realized by this probe with a 0.36 μM detection limit in the 0.6–25 μM concentration range. Most significantly, the cytotoxicity on Hela cells indicates the 4-CP-derived C-dots have a negligible cytotoxicity. The C-dots were applied in detection in environmental samples and imaging in Hela cells of Fe(III), demonstrating their good applicability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, and providing an alterative approach to totally eliminate the harm of chlorophenols (CPs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: 4-Chlorophenol (4-CP)
Carbon dots (C-dots)
Fe(III) determination
Cellular toxicity
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent response to Fe(III) of the 4-CP-derived C-dots
Selectivity for Fe(III) of the 4-CP-derived C-dots
Possible response mechanism of C-dots to Fe(III)
Applications of 4-CP-derived C-dots in real sample assay and intracellular imaging of Fe(III)
Intracellular imaging of Fe(III) based on the 4-CP-derived C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy image (A) and the power X-ray diffraction pattern (B) of the pesticide-based C-dots.
FT-IR spectrum (A), and XPS spectra of C-dots (B) and high-resolution XPS data of C1s (C) and Cl2p (D) of C-dots.

Spectral properties of pesticide-based C-dots. Normalized UV-vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of C-dots (A). Inset is the photograph taken under 365 nm UV lamp. The pH-dependent emission of C-dots (B). Inset is the plots of intensity vs pH. The effect of excitation on the emission of C-dots (C). Fluorescence decay of C-dots in aqueous solution (D).

Fluorescence spectra of Fe(III) titrating C-dots aqueous solution (A) and selectivity of C-dots chemosensor for Fe(III) over other ones in pH ∼7.4 (B). Insets in A are photographs of C-dots (1), C-dots quenched by 30 μM Fe(III) (2), respectively. Concentrations of C-dots are all 30 mg mL-1.
UV-vis spectra of C-dots, and C-dots spiked by 5.0 μM of Fe(III), and excitation and emission spectra of C-dots (A). Fluorescence decay of C-dot spiked by 5.0 μM of Fe(III) (B).

Cytotoxicity contrast of C-dots and 4-CP. Effect of concentration and exposure time of C-dots and 4-CP on HeLa cells viability (A). The effects of potential biomolecules (B) and anions (C) on fluorescence spectra of C-dots aqueous solution. The concentrations of various biomolecules from left to right are 0.2, 1000, 0.1, 12.5, 2.5, 2, 5, 4, 5, 3.2 μM, respectively.

Cellular imaging of Fe(III). (A-D) are washed Cells imaged under confocal fluorescence of C-dots before (A) and after the addition of Fe(III) (B), bright field (C), and their overlap (D), respectively. The scale bars are all 50 μm.
Schematic presentation of the preparation of chlorine-doped C-dots using 4-CP as a precursor.

head.ce:title: Pesticide-derived bright chlorine-doped carbon dots for selective determination and intracellular imaging of Fe(III)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hefei Center for Disease Control and Prevention, 218 North Susong Road, Hefei, Anhui, 230061, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by the conversion from organics or biomass to fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots), the use of pesticide 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) as a precursor to prepare C-dots has been reported. The as-prepared chlorine-doped C-dots display a brightly blue emission at ∼445 nm with ∼22.8% quantum yield. Also, the surface of C-dots enriches functional groups, such as phenolic hydroxyl and carboxylic acid, etc., which can capture ferric ion (Fe(III)), resulting in the quenching of blue fluorescence of C-dots through an inner filter effect. The quantitative assay for Fe(III) was therefore realized by this probe with a 0.36 μM detection limit in the 0.6–25 μM concentration range. Most significantly, the cytotoxicity on Hela cells indicates the 4-CP-derived C-dots have a negligible cytotoxicity. The C-dots were applied in detection in environmental samples and imaging in Hela cells of Fe(III), demonstrating their good applicability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, and providing an alterative approach to totally eliminate the harm of chlorophenols (CPs).

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) emerged as a class of superior and universal nanosized fluorophores, which have increasing attention with a rapidly growing number of publications appearing each year since 2004 [1,2]. C-dots integrated plenty of key merits, shaping their glorious application prospect in various fields. For instance, on the basis of their photoluminescence and multi-photon excitation property, a good deal of sensing platforms have been constructed for stimuli determination [3,4]. They may serve as effective carries for the targeted drug delivery, and allow simultaneous visual monitoring of releasing kinetics owing to their small size and good biocompatibility [5]. More importantly, due to the lack of intrinsic toxicity and incorporated toxic elements, C-dots owned a high potential as appropriate replacements for organic dyes and heavy metal-based quantum dots (QDs) in the fields of electronics, catalyst, and biology [6–8]. The synthesis of C-dots through simple hydrothermal treatment, carbonization and chemical oxidation, brought plenty of active groups, which, together with built-in ones of precursors, facilitated the ease of bioconjugation of C-dots. Recently, Liu’s group [9] summarized the progress of natural product derived C-dots, highlighting the carbon sources of C-dots far different from conventional luminous materials. What is more, besides cheap, abundant, renewable and biocompatible raw materials [10,11], eco-friendly natural product derived C-dots further motivated their life and vitality. For example, heteroatoms-containing natural products is beneficial to convert heteroatoms into C-dots, which not only endowed them with a wide potential in catalysis and optronics [12,13], but also led to significant improvement in their photoluminescence quantum yield (QY), figuring out the barely few percent QY of C-dots in the early years [14–19]. Therefore, the innovative synthesis, purposive functionalization and revolutionary application of C-dots are still the research hotspots.

Fe(III) is crucial in most organisms. It plays important roles in many physiological and pathological processes including oxygen uptake and transport, cellular metabolism, enzyme catalysis and electron transfer process, and the total cellular iron concentration is high to 0.3 ± 0.1 mM [20]. The paucity or overload of Fe(III) may cause several disorders and diseases, such as cell damage, anemia and Parkinson’s disease, and so on [21]. Also, the increasing amount of Fe(III) in natural aquatic systems and environmental water became a great threat for human health and aquatic organisms. Therefore, the considerable efforts have focused on the findings of outstandingly selective techniques for the determination of Fe(III). Among these, fluorescent techniques have been mainly employed for the detection of Fe(III) in biological and environmental samples due to the avoidance of complicated pretreatment procedures and the nonuse of sophisticated instrumentations, especially, simplicity and non-invasiveness. The fluorospectrophotometry-based highly sensitive materials, such as metal-organic frameworks [22,23], nobel metal nanoclusters [24–27], dye [28], up conversion nanoparticles [29], emerged in an endless stream. However, the probes constructed by above materials intrinsically suffered from poor solubility, high cytotoxicity, and bad photostability, which hinders their applications in complex systems. Most fortunately, as a next generation contrast reagent, C-dots can effectively overcome the drawbacks above, and greatly contribute the Fe(III) assay [17,18,30–40]. In view of advantages of C-dots [1,2], much more new C-dots are expected to be explored for the determination of Fe(III) in cell.

Herein a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis approach was adopted to prepare C-dots by using 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) pesticide as a precursor. The as-prepared chlorine-doped C-dots own a relatively high QY (∼22.8%) at the maximum emission of ∼445 nm. Also, the newly synthesized C-dots display pH- and excitation-dependent emission. The functional groups on the surface of C-dots, such as –OH and –COOH, etc., can coordinate with Fe(III), and the formed absorbed complex quenched the fluorescence of C-dots via an inner filter effect. On the basis of this finding, a novel nanoplatform was therefore developed for selective Fe(III) determination with detection limits of 0.36 μM, exhibiting a linear response in 0.6–25 μM concentration range. Significantly, in comparison to the high toxicity of precursor, the cytotoxicity test results indicate the pesticide-derived C-dots showed much lower cytotoxicity. These C-dots were successfully applied in Fe(III)imaging in Hela cells.

4-CP with purity over 98% is the production of J&K Chemical LTD. (Shanghai, China). Both sodium hexametaphoshpate (SHPP) with purity of 99% and thiourea with purity over 99% are purchased from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin, China). All other reagents were of analytical and used as received.

The UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) in fluorescence mode. The fluorescence lifetime assessment curves were obtained by an HORIBA FluoroHub Analytical Instrument. FT-IR spectrum using KBr pellets in the 400-4000 cm-1 region was recorded on a Nicolet-6700 spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). The size and morphology of the C-dots was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-200CX (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) microscope under a 200 kV accelerating voltage. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was obtained on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an XPS instrument (ThermoESCALAB250, USA) using Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The acidity was measured with a Sartorius PB-10 pH meter (Sartorius, Dietikon, Switzerland). Operating at laser wavelength at 633 nm and a measurement angle of 173° (backscatter detection) at 25 °C, the ζ-potential of C-dots in aqueous solution was analyzed by dynamic light scattering (Nano-Z, Malvern, UK). The confocal images were acquired by Nikon A1R-si laser scanning confocal microscopy.

Quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54, 0.1 M) as a reference, the QY of C-dots in aqueous solution was calculated according to the following Eq. (1):
(1) ϕs = ϕst(Ast/Ist) (Is/As) (ηs/ηst)2
Where ϕ is the QY, I and A represent the integrated emission intensity and the optical density, respectively. The η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts st and s denote the standard and the sample, respectively.

In brief, 0.1 g of 4-CP was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol. The solution was bubbled with N2 for 0.5 h to exclude dissolved oxygen prior to transfer to the Teflon-lined autoclave, and then was heated at 185 °C for 10 h. The C-dots solution naturally cooled to room temperature was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to the removal of large precipitates. The supernatant was concentrated by rotary evaporation at 60 °C and then re-dispersed in mixed solution of 2:1 (v/v) water and dichloromethane for 3 times to remove the residual 4-CP molecules. The purified C-dots were preserved in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The cytotoxicity of as-prepared C-dots for HeLa cells was conducted by the standard MTT assay. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 1 × 104 cells per well in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) medium for 24 h. The medium was then replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing various concentrations of C-dots and incubated for another 24 h. After that, the medium was replaced again by fresh medium (100 μL) containing MTT (20 μL, 5 mg mL-1) into each well for 4 h. Finally, the relative viabilities of the HeLa cells can be assessed through the absorption at the wavelength of 490 nm.

HeLa cells (1 × 104) were plated in a cover-glass-bottom dish in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 100 μg mL-1 penicillin/streptomycin for 24 h. The stocking medium of C-dots (100 μg mL-1) were added to HeLa cells and incubated for 4 h. The cells were washed with PBS for three times. HeLa cells were then co-cultured in DMEM supplemented with 50 μM Fe(III). After the incubation for 0.5 h, the HeLa cells were washed with cold PBS for cell imaging.

The tail water samples from Yuwan, Jianghuai automobile and Economic Development Zone waste water treatment plants were filtered through 0.45 μm Supor filters. The mother liquor was adjusted pH to 7.4 followed by being collected in a pre-cleaned glass bottle and stored in a refrigerator. A recovery test was conducted on the samples spiked with 5.0, 10.0 and 15.0 μM Fe(III) to evaluate its reliability.

On the one hand, chlorophenols (CPs) pesticide imposed toxic effects on human health, including reproductive system and metabolism of aquatic organisms, and prompted endocrine disruptions, gene mutations and DNA damages, etc., [41,42]. Therefore, it is of great necessity to find effective methods to reduce, even eradicate out the harm of CPs. On the other hand, the optical and electronic performance of C-dots can be improved by heteroatom doping [12–16,42–45]. The doped heteroatom exerted a tremendous effect on C-dots, tuning their carbon skeleton matrices and chemical structure of the surface to better the integral characteristics of C-dots [44]. Moreover, the introduction of impurity atoms into the carbon core can form p-type and n-type carriers [45]. The reported QY of N,S co-doped C-dots is high to ∼73% [16]. Both above inspired us to attempt to the use of CPs in pursuit of doped C-dots at an appropriate solvothermal condition, and the fringe benefit is to realize the significant turnaround from poison to treasure of CPs pesticides.

In the present work, 4-CP synchronously as the representative of CPs and a carbon source was utilized to synthesize C-dots. The reaction was firstly condensed among 4-CP molecules to form polymer-like composites (see Scheme 1 A). Whereupon they were carbonized to form C-dots (see Scheme 1 B). The morphology, surface charged property and crystalline state of the C-dots were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the fresh 4-CP-derived C-dots exhibited a nearly spherical morphology, and a monodispersion state and a narrow size distribution were observed. The statistical particle size of C-dots was mainly distributed in the range of 2.7–6.4 nm with an average diameter of 4.5 nm. Moreover, the 4-CP-derived C-dots showed a diffraction peak at 25.5° in a typical XRD pattern (see Fig. 1B), which indicates an interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm. This value is slightly larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm), possibly ascribing to the introduction of C-O-C, COOR etc. groups [46]. The -6.2 mV of zeta-potential of the C-dots demonstrates C-dots are less negatively charged, which can be made clear a little carboxylic groups anchored on the surface of the C-dots (see Fig. S1). Reversely, due to their relative chemical inertness, a large amount of phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the C-dots did not influence on the surface charge. The excellent water solubility of C-dots was verified by the finding as follows, when the dried C-dots were re-dispersed in water and dichloromethane mixed solvent (v/v, 1/1), high brightness was observed in the water layer, but colorlessness in dichloromethane one (datum not shown).

FTIR spectrum was further conducted to identify the surface functional groups present on C-dots. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the characteristic absorption bands of O-H stretching vibrations at 3415 cm-1, indicating the presence of many phenolic hydroxyl groups on the C-dots surface. The spectrum displays the peak at 1020 cm-1 is assigned to alkoxy C-OH stretching vibration [47]. The peak from 1370 to 1480 cm-1 and 1582 cm-1 can be attributed to COO- and aromatic C=C groups, respectively [48]. The peak at 1228 cm-1 involves the epoxide/ether C-O-C. In addition, the peak at 1645 cm-1 is ascribed to the C=O stretching vibration, and the peak at 620 cm-1 is identified as the aromatic C-Cl bonds [46]. The XPS was conducted to further examine the chemical composition and structure of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 2B, three strong binding energy peaks at 285, 199.8, and 496.54 eV attributing to C1s, Cl2p, and O1s, respectively, were observed. It can be seen from Fig. 2C, the high resolution C1s XPS spectrum displays three peaks at 285, 285.9 and 284.4 eV, which are ascribed to C-O/COOR, aromatic C-Cl, and C-C/C=C species, respectively [49]. On the high-resolution Cl2p XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 2D, two peaks at 199.9 and 200.8 eV are attributed to the aromatic C-Cl, and chlorine anion, respectively [50]. The observations from FTIR and XPS spectra revealed the phenolic hydroxyl, COOH, aromatic C-Cl, etc., decorate the surface of C-dots, giving the surface state of C-dots shown in Scheme 1B.

The UV-vis absorption, excitation- and pH-dependent photoluminescence, QY and lifetime were first investigated to learn the photophysicochemical properties of the C-dots. As shown in Fig. 3 A, in the normalized UV-vis spectrum, three typical absorption bands around 225 nm, 276 nm and 325 nm, which were attributed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic ring, n→π* transition of C=O and the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states, respectively, were observed [51]. The normalized emission spectra of C-dots reveal the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths centered at ∼351 nm and ∼445 nm, respectively. C-dots can emit brightly blue fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp (see inset in Fig. 3A). Using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY = 0.54) as a reference, the QY of C-dots in aqueous solution was fitted as ∼0.228 by the slope method (see Fig. S2). Which, together with the negligible cytotoxicity (see Section 3.6.2), signified the great potential of 4-CP-derived C-dots for target imaging in cells. As shown in Fig. 3B, when pH was adjusted from 2.0 to 11.0, the emission of C-dots firstly enhanced, and then gradually decreased with the optimal pH at ∼5.0 (see inset in Fig. 3B). Due to the varying isoelectric points and dissociation constants, ampholytes such as phenolic hydroxyl and -COOH exert tremendous effect on the emission of the C-dots [4,10,14]. The pH-dependent emission behavior of the C-dots can be assigned to these groups anchored on the surface.

As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the emission peak of C-dots red-shifted from 430 to 462 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 410 nm (see Fig. S3). It can be synchronously observed the maximum excitation wavelength centered at 351 nm, and the corresponding emission located at 445 nm, taking on first enhancement and then subsequent decrease. This excitation-dependent fluorescence emission was similar to that of carbon nanomaterials, and can be attributed to emissive traps and the electronic conjugate structure of C-dots [52]. As shown in Fig. 3D, the time-resolved decay curve was obtained by the time-correlated single photon counting method. The decay curve fits well to a tri-exponent nature as demonstrated by the residuals (see Fig. S4), and mean lifetime is calculated as 4.62 ns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response to Fe(III) of the 4-CP-derived C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of using such chlorine-doped C-dots for the determination of Fe(III) was initially considered. The C-dots showed the maximal emission at pH 5.0, and the decay in intensity was about 16% at physiologic pH (∼7.4), which is not a fatal digit that greatly influence the applications of C-dots (see inset of Fig. 3B). For the pursuit of the potential of C-dots in the fields of chemosensing and bioimaging, the pH values of all experiments in aqueous solution were buffered with Tris-HCl at 7.4, and the 30 mg mL-1 of C-dots concentration was adopted due to their considerable brightness. The effects of Fe(III) on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots were mainly explored. Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescence spectra of the 4-CP-derived C-dots dispersion after adding various Fe(III) concentrations. Upon the addition of Fe(III), a gradual decrease in intensity at 445 nm was observed. When the Fe(III) concentration is up to 30 μM, the fluorescence was quenched by ∼75%, revealing the C-dots were rather sensitive to Fe(III) and a promising candidate for the quantitative analysis of Fe(III).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for Fe(III) of the 4-CP-derived C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The complexity of the real samples imposes a great challenge to the analytical methods for metal ion detection on not only sensitivity but also selectivity. Thus, the effects of representative metal ions on the fluorescence intensity were examined under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4B, in comparison to ∼70% of quenching ratio generated by 20 μM of Fe(III), the vast majority of metal ions, such as 1.0 mM of Na+, K+, Li+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Ca2+, and 20 μM of Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+, etc., don’t clearly influence on the fluorescence intensity of the 4-CP-derived C-dots. However, copper ion exerts a slight interference to the Fe(III) detection because 20 μM of Cu2+ led to a ∼23% of fluorescence quenching. Most fortunately, thiourea as a masking agent for Cu2+ can effectively eliminate this interference [14,46]. Indeed, thiourea has no interference to the fluorescence of C-dots (see Fig. S5). The addition of Cu2+ in thiourea and C-dots co-existing aqueous solution did not result in the quenching of fluorescence intensity. Reversely, the addition of Fe3+ led to the same quenching efficiency as that in C-dots aqueous solution (data not shown). These results above indicate that the pesticide-derived C-dots exhibits high selectivity for Fe(III). Therefore, a facile, effective and selective platform for Fe(III) detection is established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of C-dots to Fe(III)
ce:section.ce:para: The previous reports [14,46,53] revealed that inner filter effect is a phenomenon as follows: if the absorption of absorbed complex partially or totally overlaps with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of fluorophore, fluorescence of fluorophore was effectively quenched. The UV-vis spectra of C-dots and Fe(III) titrating C-dots aqueous solution were studied. It can be seen from the UV-vis absorption curve of C-dots, three absorption bands located at 225, 275 and 325 nm, respectively (see Fig. 3A). Correspondingly, the addition of Fe(III) into the C-dots aqueous solution resulted in the occurrence of three new UV-vis absorption bands, two ones centered at 245, 333 nm, and a shoulder peak ranged from 400 to 600 nm (see Fig. 5 A). The new absorption bands may result from the absorbed complex formed by C-dots and Fe(III) (see Scheme 1C), which was testified by the fact that the fluorescence intensity can be most recovered when the SHPP, a much stronger chelating reagent than C-dots, was added into the mixed solution of C-dots and Fe(III) (see Fig. S6). Also, the lifetime measurement after the addition of Fe(III) into C-dots aqueous solution gave the same result (see Fig. 5B). The absorption band at 333 nm almost totally overlapped with the excitation wavelength of C-dots, and the broad shoulder one nearly covered the whole emission wavelength of C-dots. Accordingly, the quenching mechanism of the C-dots by Fe(III) can be richly explained through the inner filter effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of 4-CP-derived C-dots in real sample assay and intracellular imaging of Fe(III)

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excellent responsiveness and selectivity for Fe(III) displayed by the pesticide 4-CP-derived C-dots (see Fig. 4), it will be expected the C-dots to apply to the detection of Fe(III) in real-world samples. As Fe(III) was continuously spiked into C-dots aqueous solution, the C-dots show a linear reduction in the 0.6–26 μM Fe(III) concentration range, a linear regression equation of Y = 0.11X + 0.77 was fitted with correlation coefficient over 0.998, and the detection limit was 0.36 μM (S/N = 3), where C is the Fe(III) concentration in μM, Y is the ratio of original intensity and instant one (F0/F) of fluorescence (see Fig. S8). The reliability of pesticide-derived C-dots was verified by 2.5% of relative standard derivation (RSD) for five replicate detections of 10.0 μM of Fe(III).

It was further assessed the applicability of 4-CP-derived C-dots for the detection of Fe(III) in real-world water samples. The tail water samples from Yuwan, Jianghuai automobile and Economic Development Zone waste water treatment plants were spiked by 5.0, 10.0 and 15.0 μM of Fe(III), respectively, were analyzed for Fe(III). The dried C-dots were diluted by tail water samples to 30.0 mg L-1, and 10.0 μM of Fe(III) was injected into the as-prepared C-dots solutions, respectively. In comparison to the injection of Fe(III) into those done by DDW, the corresponding fluorescence intensities kept nearly changeless, meaning no Fe(III) in the tail water samples. Then the developed method was employed to detect the Fe(III) concentrations spiked into the tail water samples. The results are identical to the spiked Fe(III) concentrations, and the quantitative recovery ratios from 99% to 103.2% were found, demonstrating the potential applicability of pesticide-derived C-dots for Fe(III) assay in the real-world water samples (see Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of Fe(III) based on the 4-CP-derived C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to intracellular Fe(III) imaging, MTT viability assay was conducted to synchronously evaluate the cytotoxicities of the pesticide 4-CP-derived C-dots and 4-CP using HeLa cells. The results shown in Fig. 6 A indicate that 4-CP pesticide exhibits much higher cytotoxicity than C-dots with regard to every same mass concentration and exposure time. With the concentration and exposure time being increased, both C-dots and 4-CP pesticide led to the reduction of cell viability. However, the relative cell viability was low to 25% for 4-CP after a 48 h exposure with a concentration of 100 mg L-1, but for C-dots, as high as about 85%. High cell viability confirmed the low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and the great potential of the as-prepared C-dots for monitoring Fe(III) in living cells. Meanwhile, the developed approach further demonstrated the feasibility using pesticides or their residuals as carbon sources to derive functionalized bionanomaterials with low or no cytotoxicity.

On the basis of the excellent performance of C-dots expressed in vitro, the development of pesticide-derived C-dots as a bio-imaging probe for Fe(III) detection is a decent alternative. Firstly, the interferences from routine biomolecules such as calf thymus DNA, CTAB, protein (BSA), amino acids (proline, tyrosine, cysteine, glutamic, histidine and homocysteine) and peptide (glutathione) for the detection of Fe(III) must be taken into account. The investigations indicate these substances have very little effects on the Fe(III) detection (see Fig. 6B). Also, the effects of routine anions on the Fe(III) determination should be studied. Without regard to strongly reductive anions such as SO3 2-, S2- and I-, and toward a mixed solution-containing 30 mg mL-1 of C-dots and 20 μM of Fe(III), 0.05 μM of phosphate, 1 mM of Cl-, SO4 2- and NO3 -, 0.5 μM of AC- and CO3 2-, 0.5 mM of F- and Br-, 0.1 mM of ClO4 -, ClO3 -, BrO3 -, did not generate obvious interferences (see Fig. 6C). The results above indicate that the pesticide-derived C-dots exhibited, as well as low cytotoxicity, good selectivity for Fe(III).

As testified above, the pesticide-derived C-dots owned relatively high QY, excellent photostability, high water solubility and good biocompatibility, especially low cytotoxicity via carbonization at high temperature, the 4-CP-derived C-dots as an excellent fluorescent probe for bioimaging were further taken into account. Results show that, after the incubation of the Hela cells with C-dots for 4 h at 37 °C, a significant blue emission at cell membrane and cytoplasm of HeLa cells was observed. In contrast, the brightness inside the cell nucleus looked very weak [54] (see Fig. 7 A and D). After exogenous Fe(III) were introduced into the C-dots pretreated Hela cells, as displayed by experiments in vitro, almost no intracellular fluorescence was seen (see Fig. 7B), indicating that the pesticide-derived probe can be applied to image intracellular Fe(III). The bright-field transmission of Hela cells demonstrates clear cell images (see Fig. 7C). What is more, after the incubation with C-dots for several hours, it was not observed obvious morphological damage of the cells, further demonstrating the pesticide-derived C-dots are non-toxic, biocompatible, and can serve as an effective probe for intracellular Fe(III) imaging.

In summary, we attempted to use 4-CP pesticide as a precursor to prepare C-dots. The as-prepared chlorine-doped C-dots can emit brightly blue fluorescence centered at ∼445 nm with ∼22.8% QY. The C-dots displayed an excellent responsiveness and selectivity toward Fe(III) due to the formation of complex between Fe(III) and phenolic hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surface of C-dots, which can quench fluorescence of C-dots via an inner filter effect, and were applied to determine Fe(III) in tail water samples. The cellular toxicity test results show the pesticide 4-CP-derived C-dots owned much lower cytotoxicity than 4-CP molecule. Most importantly, the pesticide-derived C-dots exhibited superior optical properties, and were promisingly developed as a bio-sensing platform for monitoring the intracellular Fe(III), further demonstrating the feasibility by preparation of the C-dots to realize a significant turnaround from poison to treasure of pesticides.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural science research key project from Education Department of Anhui Province (KJ2018A0513, KJ2017A492) for financial support. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085MB40), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (41673131, 21777001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117594.
